content:
Apparently, the greatest challenge facing the Indian legal profession in 2026 is not fake lawyers, crumbling infrastructure, or unpaid juniors. It is advocates making "Instagram Reels"!

The Bar Council of India’s recent circular regulating the use of social media by advocates was, perhaps, inevitable. In an age of sensational thumbnails, “guaranteed bail” advertisements and courtroom vlogs, some intervention was always going to come. Courtrooms are not film sets, judicial proceedings are not entertainment, and no profession can survive if ethics are sacrificed at the altar of algorithms.

On that, there can be little disagreement! Yet, while reading the regulations, one cannot help but wonder whether we, as a profession, have become exceptionally good at treating symptoms while postponing conversations about the disease itself. 

For decades, junior advocates have struggled with uncertain incomes, inadequate infrastructure, lack of mentorship and difficult working conditions. Court complexes remain overcrowded, strikes and standoffs have become alarmingly common, and the same circular itself acknowledges the existence of fake lawyers and unethical practices within the system.

Curiously, however, the urgency reserved for these problems rarely matches the urgency with which social media is now being regulated. And while I agree that advocates must maintain dignity and decorum, particularly within courtrooms and other sensitive spaces, there is also a danger in treating every lawyer with a camera as a threat to the profession. 

For an entire generation of first-generation practitioners, social media has become more than a vanity project. It is where legal rights are explained in plain language, constitutional principles are discussed, and ordinary citizens are introduced to a profession that has, for far too long, appeared as mysterious and inaccessible as "Atlantis". The concern is not regulation. It is prioritisation.

The challenge, therefore, is not whether lawyers should be regulated online. They should. The challenge is whether regulation will be accompanied by equal enthusiasm for addressing the profession’s deeper structural concerns. Because if legal reform moved as swiftly as social-media regulation, perhaps junior advocates would spend less time protesting and more time practising.

And perhaps the real irony is that while lawyers are being told not to create content, many are still waiting for someone to create conditions worth staying for!

Do you think we are in a situation of misplaced priorities? Does a minor flu need more medicines than the actual epidemic? Let me know in the comments!

comment: The bigger test may be enforcement consistency. If online conduct is regulated, similar urgency is needed for improving legal aid, mentoring, and entry barriers that shape professional standards.
